question: The number of isomers possible for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Pt</mi><mfenced><mi>en</mi></mfenced><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
PtenNO22

So, total possible isomers are 3.

The existence of coordination compounds with the same formula but different arrangements of the ligands was crucial in the development of coordination chemistry. Two or more compounds with the same formula but different arrangements of the atoms are called isomers.

subject: Chemistry
q: White precipitate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> dissolves in aqueous ammonia solution due to formation of
question: White precipitate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> dissolves in aqueous ammonia solution due to formation of
solution:
The white precipitate silver chloride is soluble in ammonia and forms the complex with coordination number 2. The complex formation is shown below.

AgCl(s)+2NH3(aq)→AgNH32Cl(aq)

It is ammoniacal silver chloride.

subject: Chemistry
q:
For octahedral MnII and tetrahedral NiII complexes, consider the following statements :

(I) both the complexes can be high spin.

(II) NiII complex can very rarely be of low spin.

(III) with strong field ligands, MnII complexes can be low spin.

(IV) aqueous solution of MnII ions is yellow in color.

The correct statements are :
question:
For octahedral MnII and tetrahedral NiII complexes, consider the following statements :

(I) both the complexes can be high spin.

(II) NiII complex can very rarely be of low spin.

(III) with strong field ligands, MnII complexes can be low spin.

(IV) aqueous solution of MnII ions is yellow in color.

The correct statements are :
solution:
With weak field ligands Mn(II) will be of high spin and with strong field ligands it will be of low spin. Ni(II) tetrahedral complexes will be genrally of high spin due to sp3 hybridisation. It can very rarely be of low spin when ligand field is very strong.

Mn(II) is of light pink color in aqueous solution.

subject: Chemistry
q: An octahedral complex with the formula \(\mathrm{CoCl}_3 \cdot \mathrm{nNH}_3\) upon reaction with excess of \(\mathrm{AgNO}_3\) solution gives 2 moles of \(\mathrm{AgCl}\). Consider the oxidation state of \(\mathrm{Co}\) in the complex is ' \(x\) '. The value of " \(x+n\) " is ______
question: An octahedral complex with the formula \(\mathrm{CoCl}_3 \cdot \mathrm{nNH}_3\) upon reaction with excess of \(\mathrm{AgNO}_3\) solution gives 2 moles of \(\mathrm{AgCl}\). Consider the oxidation state of \(\mathrm{Co}\) in the complex is ' \(x\) '. The value of " \(x+n\) " is ______
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}+0-1-2=0 \\ & \mathrm{x}=+3 \\ & \mathrm{n}=5 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{n}=8\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Number of complexes which show optical isomerism among the following is _________.

cis-Cr(ox)2Cl23-,Co(en)33+, cis -Pt(en)2Cl22+, cis -Co(en)2Cl2+

​​​​​​​,trans-Pt(en)2Cl22+, trans -Cr(ox)2Cl23-
question:
Number of complexes which show optical isomerism among the following is _________.

cis-Cr(ox)2Cl23-,Co(en)33+, cis -Pt(en)2Cl22+, cis -Co(en)2Cl2+

​​​​​​​,trans-Pt(en)2Cl22+, trans -Cr(ox)2Cl23-
solution:
Pt(en)2Cl22+ is a square planar complex forms geometrical isomers (cis and trans). Trans isomer does not show optical isomerism since it is symmetrical, while cis isomer shows optical isomerism as it is unsymmetrical.

POS = plane of symmetry

COS = centre of symmetry

The compounds,  cis−[Co(en)2Cl2]+ and [Co(en)3]3+ show optical isomerism as they do not possess any element of symmetry.

cis-Cr(ox)2Cl23-→can show optical isomerism (no POS & COS)

trans-Pt(en)2Cl22+→can't show optical isomerism (contain POS & COS)

trans-Cr(ox)2Cl23-→can't show optical isomerism(contain POS & COS)

Hence, among the given complexes 4 complexes can show optical isomerism.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
List-I
Electronic configuration of tetrahedral metal ion		List-II
Crystal Field Stabilization Energy ($\Delta_t$)
A. $d^2$I. $-0.6$
B. $d^4$II. $-0.8$
C. $d^6$III. $-1.2$
D. $d^8$IV. $-0.4$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
List-I
Electronic configuration of tetrahedral metal ion		List-II
Crystal Field Stabilization Energy ($\Delta_t$)
A. $d^2$I. $-0.6$
B. $d^4$II. $-0.8$
C. $d^6$III. $-1.2$
D. $d^8$IV. $-0.4$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
In a tetrahedral crystal field, the d-orbitals split into a lower energy $e$ set and a higher energy $t_2$ set. The energy of an electron in the $e$ orbital is $-0.6 \Delta_t$ and in the $t_2$ orbital is $+0.4 \Delta_t$. Since $\Delta_t$ is generally small, tetrahedral complexes form high-spin configurations.

The Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) is given by:
$\text{CFSE} = (-0.6 n_e + 0.4 n_{t_2}) \Delta_t$

For $d^2$: The configuration is $e^2 t_2^0$.
$\text{CFSE} = 2 \times (-0.6) + 0 = -1.2 \Delta_t$ (A $\rightarrow$ III)

For $d^4$: The configuration is $e^2 t_2^2$.
$\text{CFSE} = 2 \times (-0.6) + 2 \times (0.4) = -1.2 + 0.8 = -0.4 \Delta_t$ (B $\rightarrow$ IV)

For $d^6$: The configuration is $e^3 t_2^3$.
$\text{CFSE} = 3 \times (-0.6) + 3 \times (0.4) = -1.8 + 1.2 = -0.6 \Delta_t$ (C $\rightarrow$ I)

For $d^8$: The configuration is $e^4 t_2^4$.
$\text{CFSE} = 4 \times (-0.6) + 4 \times (0.4) = -2.4 + 1.6 = -0.8 \Delta_t$ (D $\rightarrow$ II)

Therefore, the correct match is A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II.

Answer: A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II

question: The d-electronic configuration of  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><msub><mi>CoCl</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> in tetrahedral crystal field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msup><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msubsup></math>. The sum of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and number of unpaired electrons is
solution:
The oxidation state of Co in complex CoCl42- is

x-4=-2

x=+2

Electronic configuration of Co is  C27o=Ar4s23d7

So, Co+2=[Ar]3d7

Since Cl- is a weak field ligand, so the given complex will have a tetrahedral geometry with following Crystal-field splitting

 

So, value of (m)+(unpaired electrons) is (4)+(3)=7.

solution:
FeCN63- In this the central metal Fe is in +3 oxidation state. And CN- is strong field ligand so, it will form inner orbital complex.

Electronic configuration in Fe3+: d5

Electronic configuration for inner orbital complex: t52g e0g

CFSE=5-0.4Δ0=-2.0Δ0

So, final answer will be =2

q: Identity the incorrect pair from the following:
question: Identity the incorrect pair from the following:
solution:
Wacker process:

The process of oxidation of terminal alkenes to aldehydes and ketones by using Palladium and Copper compounds as catalysts is called the Wacker process.

Palladium (II) chloride and Copper (II) chloride are used in this process along with Oxygen and water.

CH2=CH2 →O2,H2OPdCl2,CuCl2CH3CHO

The catalyst used in Wacker's process is PdCl2

solution:
K3CuCN4:

CuCN4-3

Oxidation number of of copper is Cu+1

Electronic configuration of Cu+1=Ar3 d104s0

Due to the absence of unpaired electrons, their spin only magnetic moment is zero, they are diamagnetic in nature

K2CuCN4:

CuCN42-, Cu2+=Ar3d9 one unpaired electron is present.Hence, paramagnetic in nature.

FeCl6-4, Fe+2: Ar 3d6 4s0,one unpaired electron.Hence, paramagnetic in nature.

q: The sum of oxidation state of the metals in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>CO</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>5</mn></msub></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>VO</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>WO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is
question: The sum of oxidation state of the metals in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>CO</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>5</mn></msub></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>VO</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>WO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is
solution:
In the complex Fe(CO)5, take the oxidation number of Fe be x . Carbonyl is a neutral ligand which doesn't have any charge. So the oxidation number of Fe would be

x-0×5 = 0= 0 

Hence, the oxidation number of iron in this complex is 0.

let oxidation state of vanadium in VO2+ = x.

so,      x + (-2) = +2  ⇒x = +2 + 2 = +4

Tungsten trioxide consists of one tungsten atom and three oxygen atoms. Tungsten is a d-block metal from group  6 and has an oxidation state +6 in the compound.

Then, Sum of oxidation state of metals in the given compounds =10

subject: Chemistry
q: The coordination geometry around the manganese in decacarbonyldimanganese0
question: The coordination geometry around the manganese in decacarbonyldimanganese0
solution:
Around the manganese atom, there are five CO ligands and one Mn occupying octahedron vertices. For octahedral geometry, the more appropriate hybridization is d2sp3 that produces six hybrid orbitals pointing towards CO and Mn.

The octahedral shape of molecules is the shape of molecules where six atoms or ligands or groups of atoms are arranged in a systematic way around a central dogma or atom. The Octahedral Shape of Molecules molecules has eight faces.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) of $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$ is greater than that of $\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$.
Statement II: Potassium ferricyanide has a greater spin-only magnetic moment than sodium ferrocyanide.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) of $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$ is greater than that of $\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$.
Statement II: Potassium ferricyanide has a greater spin-only magnetic moment than sodium ferrocyanide.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
$\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+} \Rightarrow$ CFSE value is zero because of $\mathrm{d}^5$ configuration with Weak Field Ligands in coordination number 6 $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$.

$\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+} \Rightarrow \mathrm{CFSE}$ value is $-0.6 \Delta_0$ because of $\mathrm{d}^4$ configuration with Weak Field Ligands in coordination number 6.

For : $\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right], \mu=\sqrt{1(1+2)}=\sqrt{3}$ B.M.

For : $\mathrm{Na}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right], \mu=\sqrt{0}$ B.M.

subject: Chemistry
q: The calculated spin-only magnetic moments <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> of the anionic and cationic species of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mfenced close="]" open="["> <mrow> <mtext>Fe</mtext><msub> <mrow> <mfenced> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>H</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msub> <mtext>O</mtext></mrow> </mfenced></mrow> <mn>6</mn> </msub> </mrow> </mfenced></mrow> <mrow> <mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mtext>Fe </mtext><msub><mfenced><mtext>CN</mtext></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> respectively, are:
question: The calculated spin-only magnetic moments <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> of the anionic and cationic species of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mfenced close="]" open="["> <mrow> <mtext>Fe</mtext><msub> <mrow> <mfenced> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>H</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msub> <mtext>O</mtext></mrow> </mfenced></mrow> <mn>6</mn> </msub> </mrow> </mfenced></mrow> <mrow> <mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mtext>Fe </mtext><msub><mfenced><mtext>CN</mtext></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> respectively, are:
solution:
In both cases iron exhibits +2 oxidation state.

Electronic configuration of F e +2 ion =[Ar]3d6

In [Fe(H2O)6]2+, H2O is a weak field ligand and hence, no pairing up.

∴ number of unpaired electron n=4

⇒Spin magnetic moment =4(4+2)=24=4.9 BM

In [Fe(CN)6]4-, CN- is a strong field ligand and hence, pairing will occur.

∴ number of unpaired electron n=0

⇒ spin magnetic moment = zero

subject: Chemistry
q:
A chromium complex with a formula $\mathrm{CrCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ has a spin only magnetic moment value of 3.87 BM and its solution conductivity corresponds to $1: 2$ electrolyte. 2.75 g of the complex solution was initially passed through a cation exchanger. The solution obtained after the process was reacted with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$. The amount of AgCl formed in the above process is $\_\_\_\_$ g. (Nearest integer)
[Given: Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{Cr}: 52 ; \mathrm{Cl}: 35.5, \mathrm{Ag}: 108, \mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{H}: 1$]
question:
A chromium complex with a formula $\mathrm{CrCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ has a spin only magnetic moment value of 3.87 BM and its solution conductivity corresponds to $1: 2$ electrolyte. 2.75 g of the complex solution was initially passed through a cation exchanger. The solution obtained after the process was reacted with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$. The amount of AgCl formed in the above process is $\_\_\_\_$ g. (Nearest integer)
[Given: Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{Cr}: 52 ; \mathrm{Cl}: 35.5, \mathrm{Ag}: 108, \mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{H}: 1$]
solution:
The complex $\text{CrCl}_3 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ with magnetic moment 3.87 BM indicates $3.87 = \sqrt{n(n+2)}$ giving n = 3 unpaired electrons, confirming Cr³⁺.
A 1:2 electrolyte means structure is $[\text{Cr(H}_2\text{O})_5\text{Cl}]^{2+}\text{Cl}_2^-$.
Molar mass = 52 + 5(18) + 35.5 + 6(18) = 285.5 g/mol.
Moles of complex = 2.75/285.5 = 0.00963 mol.
After cation exchange, 2 Cl⁻ remain per formula unit (from the 1:2 electrolyte), giving 0.00963 × 2 = 0.01926 mol of free Cl⁻.
Reacting with excess AgNO₃: mass of AgCl = 0.01926 × 143.5 = 2.76 g ≈ 3 g (nearest integer).

subject: Chemistry
q: Identify the metal ions among $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}, \mathrm{V}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Ti}^{2+}$ having a spin-only magnetic moment value more than 3.0 BM. The sum of unpaired electrons present in the high spin octahedral complexes formed by those metal ions is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Identify the metal ions among $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}, \mathrm{V}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Ti}^{2+}$ having a spin-only magnetic moment value more than 3.0 BM. The sum of unpaired electrons present in the high spin octahedral complexes formed by those metal ions is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
The spin-only magnetic moment is given by $\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)}$ BM, where $n$ is the number of unpaired electrons.
For $\mu > 3.0$ BM, $n$ must be $\ge 3$ (since for $n=2$, $\mu = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83$ BM and for $n=3$, $\mu = \sqrt{15} \approx 3.87$ BM).
Electronic configurations and unpaired electrons ($n$) in high spin octahedral complexes:
Metal IonConfiguration$n$$\mu$ (BM)
$Co^{2+}$$[Ar] 3d^7$33.87
$Ni^{2+}$$[Ar] 3d^8$22.83
$Fe^{2+}$$[Ar] 3d^6$44.90
$V^{3+}$$[Ar] 3d^2$22.83
$Ti^{2+}$$[Ar] 3d^2$22.83

The metal ions with $\mu > 3.0$ BM are $Co^{2+}$ ($n=3$) and $Fe^{2+}$ ($n=4$).
The sum of unpaired electrons for these ions is $3 + 4 = 7$.

q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : The identification of Ni2+ is carried out by dimethyl glyoxime in the presence of NH4OH.

Statement II :The dimethyl glyoxime is a bidentate neutral ligand.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : The identification of Ni2+ is carried out by dimethyl glyoxime in the presence of NH4OH.

Statement II :The dimethyl glyoxime is a bidentate neutral ligand.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true.
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false.
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true.
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false.
solution:
Neutral dimethyl glyoxime does not act as ligand.

When Ni2+ reacts with dimethyl glyoxime in presence of NH4OH, it produce dimethyl glyoximate then it form rozy red ppt.

Niaq2++2dmg-→Nidmg2Rosy red ppt.

subject: Chemistry
q: The IUPAC name of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>[</mo><mi>Co</mi><mo>(</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>]</mo></math> is:
question: The IUPAC name of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>[</mo><mi>Co</mi><mo>(</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>]</mo></math> is:
solution:
Ligands that include a numerical prefix in the name use the prefixes bis for 2, tris for 3, or tetrakis for 4 to indicate their number.

If the complex ion is an anion, we drop the ending of the metal name and add –ate.

In the given coordination compound, Co is the cobalt metal, C2O42- are the ligands named as oxalato.

The charge on [Co(C2O4)3] is -3 and oxalate is having charge of -2.

X+3(-2)=-3X=+3

Hence, the IUPAC name of the compound K3[Co(C2O4)3] is Potassium trioxalatocobaltate(III).

subject: Chemistry
q:
The spin-only magnetic moment value of $\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n+-}}$ ion formed among $\mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Zn} \mathrm{Mn}$ and Cu that has the least enthalpy of atomisation is ________. (in nearest integer)
Here n is equal to the number of diamagnetic complexes among $\mathrm{K}_2\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right],\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$,
$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] \text { and }\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{PPh}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{I}\right]$
question:
The spin-only magnetic moment value of $\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n+-}}$ ion formed among $\mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Zn} \mathrm{Mn}$ and Cu that has the least enthalpy of atomisation is ________. (in nearest integer)
Here n is equal to the number of diamagnetic complexes among $\mathrm{K}_2\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right],\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$,
$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] \text { and }\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{PPh}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{I}\right]$
solution:
$\mathrm{K}_2\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3$, Paramagnetic
$\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2$, Diamagnetic
$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{sp}^3$, Paramagnetic
$\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{PPh}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{I}\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3$, Diamagnetic
Hence the value of $n$ is 2
Least value of enthalpy of atomisation among Ni ,
$\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Mn}$ and Cu is of Zn
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Zn}^{+2}:-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10} \\
& \mu=0
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is diamagnetic ?
question: Which of the following is diamagnetic ?
solution: $\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$ has magnetic moment of a single unpaired electron whereas $\left[\mathrm{FeF}_6\right]^{3-}$ has a magnetic moment of five unpaired electrons. $\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}$ is paramagnetic with four unpaired electrons while $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^3$ is diamagnetic. This anomaly is explained by valence bond theory in terms of formation of inner and outer orbital coordination entities. $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-}$ is an inner orbital complexes having $d^2 s p^3$ hybridization.

subject: Chemistry
q: The octahedral complex of a metal ion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> with four monodentate ligands <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math><sub> </sub>absorb wavelengths in the region of red, green, yellow and blue, respectively. The increasing order of ligand strength of the four ligands is
question: The octahedral complex of a metal ion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> with four monodentate ligands <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math><sub> </sub>absorb wavelengths in the region of red, green, yellow and blue, respectively. The increasing order of ligand strength of the four ligands is
solution:
The colors of wavelengths are absorbed in decreasing order of wavelengths.

λ red > λ yellow > λ green > λ blue

as ΔE=hcλ

Δ E Red < Δ E Yellow < Δ E Green < Δ E Blue

L1 < L3 < L2 < L4

As the order of ligand field strength provides the order of relative crystal field splitting energies.
